region_of_origin: Goa, India; widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Fish Vindaloo; closest defensible match is fish curry (FOODON_03315595). Vindaloo is a distinct preparation with vinegar and chilli but falls within the fish curry category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fish dishes broadly; no vindaloo-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. This is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for fish vindaloo or vindaloo-style fish dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Fish Vindaloo is approximately 250 g, representing one restaurant or home-cooked portion inclusive of fish pieces, potatoes, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Vindaloo — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Fish Vindaloo (cr_id G00010265). All per-100g macros, fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central fish curry analogues as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Curry-Based Foods — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (potato-containing curry with vinegar)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Fish Vindaloo exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the closest analogous entry: potato-containing curry dishes with vinegar. Vinegar is documented in peer-reviewed literature (Johnston et al., 2004, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition) to reduce postprandial glucose response by approximately 20–30%, which lowers the effective GI of potato-based dishes from the mid-60s range into the low GI range (below 55). Carbohydrate content is 12.33 g/100g; GL per 250 g serving = (50 × 12.33 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 7.7 g. This is classified as low GL. GI category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 383 mg/100g is borderline high; partially offset by potassium 292 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.4 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request a reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100g, monitor cumulative sodium from accompaniments such as rice or bread, and note that the vinegar-based preparation and high potassium content provide a partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 12.33 g/100g moderated by dietary fibre 3.4 g/100g and vinegar, which reduces postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish Vindaloo is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 250 g portion without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; the vinegar-based preparation and fibre content moderate glucose absorption, and the estimated GI of 50 places this dish in the low GI range, supporting glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids; total fat is moderate at 5.65 g/100g with estimated saturated fat of 1.2 g/100g; anti-inflammatory spices present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish Vindaloo is generally suitable for cardiovascular health due to the omega-3 content from fish, low saturated fat, and anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric, ginger, and cumin; however, sodium at 383 mg/100g should be monitored and a reduced-oil preparation is preferred to limit total fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from fish and anti-inflammatory polyphenols from vinegar, turmeric, ginger, and cumin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Vindaloo may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to omega-3 fatty acids and anti-inflammatory spice compounds; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) should be monitored for individual joint sensitivity, and a reduced-oil preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Vindaloo is a very spicy, tangy fish curry originating from Goa, India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish is prepared by marinating fish — commonly mackerel, pomfret, or a firm white fish — in a paste of vinegar, dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, turmeric, cumin, and coriander, then slow-cooking it with potatoes and tomatoes in oil. The vinegar is the defining ingredient, giving the dish its characteristic sharp, sour heat that distinguishes it from other Indian fish curries. The texture is saucy and rich, with tender fish pieces and soft potatoes absorbing the spiced gravy. Nutritionally, Fish Vindaloo provides 111 kcal per 100g, with 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat, and 12.33 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100g and potassium at 292 mg/100g are notable. Sodium is 383 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The vinegar content is clinically relevant as it moderates postprandial glucose response, making this dish more suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes than its carbohydrate content alone would suggest. The omega-3 fatty acids from fish and anti-inflammatory spice compounds support cardiovascular and joint health. In Singapore, Fish Vindaloo is typically served with steamed rice or roti prata and is a popular lunch and dinner option.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for an exact match on Fish Vindaloo (cr_id G00010265). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central fish curry analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework with peer-reviewed vinegar-effect evidence placing the effective GI in the low range.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central fish curry analogues (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 35188 and related entries); HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Fish Vindaloo are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo; this is confirmed zero per the source and is plausible for a fish-based dish where the specific fish type and preparation may result in negligible cholesterol in the analysed sample — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. 4. GI estimated at 50 (low, GI ≤ 55) using analogous potato-containing curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; vinegar effect documented in Johnston et al. (2004), European Journal of Clinical Nutrition, reduces effective GI by approximately 20–30% from the mid-60s baseline of potato-based dishes into the low GI range. GL per 250 g serving = (50 × 12.33 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 7.7 g (low GL). 5. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. Fish Vindaloo is of Goan Indian origin but is widely served in Singapore and classified under Singapore Indian cuisine for multilingual coverage purposes. 6. Halal status marked true as fish is halal by default in Islamic dietary law; individual restaurant preparation should be verified.
